risks: 1. Sprite identification is manual and error-prone — we need to figure out which of ~190 numbered singles are animation frames of the same object vs. distinct objects. Wrong groupings mean broken animations or missing furniture.

2. Performance regression from animated furniture — if every animated object creates its own render cycle or canvas operation each frame, a scene with 15+ animated items could tank FPS. Must batch properly and only re-draw changed frames.

3. Z-sorting interaction — animated furniture that changes visual bounds (e.g., a fan spinning wider than its base tile) could break the existing z-sort pipeline if sprite dimensions vary between frames.
over_engineering_flags: Don't build a generic animation state machine for furniture — furniture animations are simple loops (no transitions, no states). A frame-cycling function using the existing identity.time pattern is sufficient.
Don't build an asset discovery system that auto-detects animation groups at runtime — a static mapping of animation groups (built once from investigating the singles) is simpler and deterministic.
Don't add a separate animation config file format — inline the frame data in the tileset-loader or a simple constant object.
challenges.recommendation: Simplify slightly. Skip any runtime auto-detection of animation frames. Instead: (1) manually investigate the singles directory to identify animation groups, (2) hardcode a static animation map, (3) extend tileset-loader to load frame arrays for animated objects, (4) use existing identity.time pattern to cycle frames in the renderer. This is one cohesive project — investigation, system, and application are tightly coupled and touch the same files.

projects.title: Furniture Animation System — Investigation, Implementation, and Scene Integration
projects.scope_summary: Investigate Limezu Modern Office singles to identify which numbered sprites form animation sequences for the same object (screens, fans, fish tanks, etc.). Build a furniture animation system that groups these frames, loads them as frame arrays in the tileset-loader, and cycles through them using the existing identity.time clock. Apply animations to all relevant furniture in the office scene. Must maintain z-sort compatibility, cache animated frames like character sprites, and avoid separate timers.
